---
title: "Journal Entry 7: Overall Project Reflection"
date: "2026-05-01"
categories: [reflection, conclusion]
description: "Overall reflection on the research project and next steps"
---

![Project reflection](../thumbnail.jpg)

## Overall Project Reflection

This final entry steps back from any single chapter or deliverable and looks at the project as a whole. MaintAlign started this semester as a vague idea about scheduling and ended as a working solver, a Streamlit dashboard, a five-chapter report, and a three-minute talk at the Conference on Emerging Research. The path between those two points was less linear than I expected.

### What did you learn?

The technical skills I developed are easiest to list. I learned constraint programming through Google's OR-Tools CP-SAT solver, including how to model optional interval variables, half-reified Booleans for conditional cost terms, and search strategies that exploit problem structure. I learned reliability modeling well enough to use Weibull distributions and Kijima Type I virtual age for imperfect repair, neither of which I had heard of before this project. I learned Monte Carlo simulation as a validation method, particularly the use of VaR95 to capture tail risk that mean cost alone hides. I picked up enough Streamlit to build a dashboard that survived a live demo, enough Quarto and LaTeX to produce a properly formatted academic report, and enough GitHub Actions to set up automated PDF builds and a CI workflow for the prototype.

The research methods were less obvious to me at the start but turned out to matter more. Designing experiments that report variance across multiple seeds rather than a single number, choosing baselines that represent honest comparisons rather than easy wins, and reporting FEASIBLE results alongside OPTIMAL ones are all habits I did not have in the fall. I also learned how to do a literature review under a constraint — the ACM-only requirement forced me to read more carefully and accept that the related-work chapter would emphasize constraint-programming-for-scheduling rather than the broader maintenance optimization tradition.

What I learned about myself as a researcher is that I overclaim by default and need explicit checks to push back against it. The first draft of every chapter overstated what the experiments showed. Without Professor Kapfhammer's feedback I would have left the report saying MaintAlign "scales optimally" rather than "scales to ten machines in under three minutes," and the difference between those two phrasings is the difference between a paper that is honest and one that is not. Knowing this about myself is useful because it means I can build the discipline into my workflow rather than relying on luck or external review to catch it.

### What challenges did you face?

The biggest obstacle was the scalability ceiling at ten machines. I had hoped to push cleanly to twenty or beyond, and I spent a long time trying tighter bounds, search strategies, and symmetry breaking before accepting that the monolithic solver simply cannot handle larger instances inside a 180-second budget. The way I worked through it was to reframe the limitation as a finding rather than a failure: ten machines is the edge of what CP-SAT does well on this problem, and the decomposition module is a partial answer for larger sizes. That framing carried through into the limitations section of @sec-limitations and made the future work section more concrete.

A second obstacle was the literature review constraint. Allegheny requires ACM Digital Library sources, and ACM's coverage of maintenance optimization is sparse compared to industrial engineering and reliability journals. I worked around it by leaning on ACM-published work in adjacent areas — constraint programming for scheduling, simulation-based validation — and by being explicit in the limitations section that the related-work chapter is narrower than a full survey would be.

A third challenge was specific to the writing process. Drafts that came out smoothly often turned out to read like AI-generated prose: uniform paragraph structure, smooth connective transitions, balanced coverage of every angle. After several rounds of feedback I started rewriting transitional phrases in my own voice before submitting anything. The fix was not a one-time edit but a habit of reading drafts aloud and asking whether each sentence sounded like a sentence I would actually say.

If I could start over, I would begin the report in the first weeks of the semester instead of treating it as a late-semester deliverable. The chapters that came out best — Chapter 4 in particular, written from real CSV data with the experiments fresh in mind — were the ones where the writing happened close to the work it described. The chapters that needed the most revision were the ones I tried to write retrospectively. I would also be more disciplined about scoping out features I cannot validate. The dashboard error handling, for example, was added late and is still incomplete; if I had committed to it earlier, it would be in the final release rather than in the future work section.

### What are you most proud of?

The result I am most proud of is that the report tells a true story. The 18 to 25 percent savings figure is hedged with the synthetic-data caveat, the scalability claim is bounded at ten machines, and the comparison to the four baselines is honest about those baselines being non-adversarial. None of those qualifications were obvious to me in the fall, and the version of this report that I would have written without feedback would have been less honest and less useful.

The Conference on Emerging Research presentation was the moment the project came together for me. Compressing a semester of work into three minutes for a non-specialist audience forced clarity I had not yet found in the longer form, and the questions I got afterward shaped how I framed the contributions in @sec-summary-results. The Streamlit dashboard mattered a lot here too. Without it, the talk would have been all equations and bar charts; with it, I could show a Gantt chart updating live as the solver ran.

The contribution I think will hold up best is the chain grouping mechanism. The half-reified Boolean encoding is a small piece of the formulation, but the cost breakdowns showed that it is responsible for a meaningful share of the savings on chain-heavy instances. That is the kind of finding that another researcher could pick up and reuse, and it is the part of the project I would most want to defend if challenged.

### What are your next steps?

The clearest next step is the senior project. The scaling study at twenty-five, fifty, and one hundred machines is the most tractable extension, since most of the infrastructure is already in place and the gap to fill is mostly compute time and a clean decomposition baseline. The Gurobi head-to-head is also high on my list and would be a strong companion to the scaling study, but it requires a license and a careful translation of the CP-SAT formulation into MIP form. I will discuss both options with Professor Kapfhammer during senior project planning.

A parallel next step is the ECON-585 health economics framing, which reuses the MaintAlign machinery for medical equipment maintenance rather than industrial machines. The reliability framework transfers directly — a CT scanner has Weibull failure characteristics in the same way a CNC lathe does — but the cost structure is different, and the ethical considerations around safety-optimized equipment become more central. That extension is closer to a case study than new technical work, but it is a useful exercise in showing that the same model applies to a domain where the stakes are not measured in dollars alone.

The questions that remain unanswered are mostly about the behavior of the solver beyond the regimes I tested. I do not know how the cost savings change with real industrial data, with adversarially-strong baselines, or at scales above twenty machines. Each of those is a separate research project in itself, and the report tries to be clear that answering any of them is future work rather than a settled result.

Beyond this specific project, the lesson I will carry into future research is that the discipline of writing things down honestly is what turns a coding exercise into research. The solver was the easy part. Deciding what the solver actually showed, and saying so without overstating, was the hard part — and it is the part most worth doing well.
